message.content: Adversaries may achieve persistence on Unix-like systems by modifying RC scripts, which are executed during system startup. These scripts, such as rc.local and rc.common, require root privileges to alter and can be used to run malicious binaries or commands upon reboot. This technique is particularly effective on lightweight Unix-like distributions, including IoT or embedded systems, which often use the root user by default. Although many Unix-like systems have transitioned to Systemd, some, like older macOS versions and certain Linux distributions, still support RC scripts for backward compatibility. Notable malware exploiting this technique includes Cyclops Blink, Green Lambert, HiddenWasp, and iKitten. Mitigation strategies include migrating to Systemd, monitoring and restricting modifications to RC scripts, and ensuring proper file permissions to prevent unauthorized changes. Regular audits and integrity checks of startup scripts can also help detect and prevent malicious alterations.
